A weekly audio discussion with Dr. Ivan Misner, the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, the world's largest business networking organization.

Episode 289: Thank You for Closed Business

January 16, 2013 by Ivan Misner 26 Comments

Podcast: Download

Subscribe: Apple Podcasts | RSS | Subscription Options

Print This Episode

Synopsis

This week John Meyer, U.S. National Director of Field Affairs for BNI and Executive Director of BNI Ohio, joins Dr. Misner on the podcast to talk about the Thank You for Closed Business (TYCB) system, which measures the business passed between BNI members.

Why do we report closed business on TYCB slips?

  • We want to thank the people who give us referrals that turn into business and provide recognition.
  • We want to make sure that members pass qualified referrals.
  • We want to maintain accountability within the chapter.

 What is reported on TYCB slips?

This varies by type of business, but in general it’s the gross income to the BNI member as the result of the referral. For more details, click here to download the “Thank You for Closed Business” Explanation PDF.

How is TYCB reported?

You can either fill out a green TYCB slip at a meeting or online through BNI Connect, where you can also track your own closed business. If you are not on BNI Connect, or if the Member Module has not been enabled in your region, talk to your Executive Director.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of BNI Podcast Episode 289

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan. How are you?

Ivan:
I am doing great, Priscilla, thank you very much. I have a special guest with us here today, John Meyer. John is the US National Director for BNI. He is also the Executive Director for BNI Ohio, where he has under his management 150 chapters of BNI throughout Ohio with roughly 35 members. John is a contributing author to the NY Times bestselling Masters of Networking. He is an avid golfer and loves to travel. He is married to his lovely wife, whom I have met many times, Victoria. He has three stepchildren. John is on the Board of Directors for Davis College in Toledo, and he is also a member of the Business Industry Advisory Council in Ohio and has been involved with BNI now for almost 20 years.

I get so excited when I see Directors and members who have been on that length of time.

John, this is the first podcast that you are on. It’s a real pleasure to have you on. Thank you for all you do for BNI. You’ve made a big difference in the organization.

John:
Thanks Ivan. It’s good to be here. I am looking forward to this.

Ivan:
We have an interesting topic today. The topic is “thank you for closed business.” Some chapters may see it occasionally as TYFCB. That stands for thank you for closed business. For those groups, and there are some, who haven’t started using this process, can you start, John, by just telling everyone briefly what is the thank you for closed business system?

John:
I will. I would love to educate some people on this. This is a very near and dear topic to me. Thank you for closed business is simply a mechanism that we use in BNI to track closed business between members. We have a slips program that we can talk about, but ultimately, this is the way that we track how successful our members are in passing referrals.

Ivan:
Measuring is important. You basically achieve what you measure in business. That’s why it is important to do this.

John:
Absolutely. If it gets measured, it gets done and that is what we tend to find out in setting standards and expectations within chapters. We want to put forth the right expectations of our members.

Ivan:
So there are three things that I would like you to talk about: why we report it, what is reported, and how we report it. So let’s start with why do we report it- these things with these slips?

John:
We report it for a number of reasons. First and foremost, we want to thank the people who give us referrals that turn into business. A lot of times, people don’t actually have a formal way to thank them. This is a way to recognize the people who pass quality referrals in the chapter.

We also want to provide the recognition that they would receive then in the chapter. The whole givers gain mentality. People will see that they are giving good quality referrals that turn into business, so people will want to start to do business with them. And it’s about accountability. We want to make sure that members are passing qualified referrals. That it is not just information that is being passed or leads being passed. These are actual referrals, and thank you for closed business shows and reports the closed business going back and forth.

“Why?” has a multitude of reasons. The biggest one is thanking referral sources, recognition, and to keep the accountability within the chapter.

Ivan:
You have done a lot of work with the team at BNI HQ to develop the BNI slips program description. We have a pretty final version of that out, do we not?

John:
We do and it has actually been incorporated into the BNI Connect system as well.

Ivan:
For this podcast, I will include a PDF that people can download and take a look at. So if you have never heard of thank you for closed business, a description will be attached to this podcast. Take a look at it as we are talking.

So what exactly is reported, John? You talked about what it is and why we report it, but what is reported?

John:
That is the million dollar question that everybody asks. It is really somewhat of a gray area, but it is based on the type of business that you are in. In the document that you are going to provide with this, it covers the different types of businesses and what you are supposed to report for thank you for closed business.

Real briefly, if you are in a commission based business, you put down your gross commission figure. Service providers, you put down the gross amount you charged. Product providers- for example, promotional products, printing, those types of industries- you put down the gross product sales. The challenging one is banking. Bankers are kind of a unique breed, so we have stipulated what you should report. The whole key here is that we generate consistency here in the organization.

Ivan:
That’s the whole thing right there, consistency. Even if someone disagrees with the way it has been defined, what is key is that we be consistent. We know that there are some challenges like banking that you mentioned. But that consistency helps address the overall strategy.

That was important, by the way, on my doctoral dissertation, in which we tracked the value of business. One of the things that the professor said was everybody is always going to disagree with how you define a referral. The key is that you have a consistent application so that it is being done the same way worldwide.

John:
There were a lot of variations of what was reported and how it was reported. We just decided that we need to make this uniform throughout the organization and have everybody report on a level playing field. I believe that is what we have attended.

Ivan:
If I had to give a brief definition of how to measure it, is it still safe to say that you really need to be taking a look at the gross income to the business? So if you open up a bank account, that is not the income for the bank. It is whatever their investment is. If you get a mortgage, it is the money that you make from it. If you are a real estate agent, it is the commission that you get. Is that still a decent way of giving a brief synopsis of what you are measuring?

John:
Yes, absolutely. The reason we do it is fairly simple. If you look at if from a visitor perspective as well, a visitor comes into a chapter and they want to see what is in it for them. They don’t care what the broker who the real estate agent reports to is making. They want to know what is going into the Realtor’s pocket. When you talk about the commission, that is what the actual member is making when we get to that point.

That’s the main thing. We don’t want to inflate the numbers. We want to have realistic numbers that we can back.

Ivan:
For example, buying a home, if the home sells for $750,000, that is really not the value of the referral. It is the value of the sale in terms of buying the house, but that is not how much someone made on it. We don’t want to inflate the numbers. It is really complex.

For those of you listening to it, we are trying to make it simple, but it is really complicated and people disagree. We look at the income to the individual as a starting point and then the devil is in the details, and this document attached to this podcast will help explain in more detail. I know you spent a lot of time on this issue, John, and you really came up with a document that is invaluable.

John:
There was a great team on it and it was a lot of fun doing it, believe it or not.

Ivan:
We are almost out of time. There is another topic. How is it reported?

John:
Very simply. There is a process in the chapter with the green slips. The thank you for closed business slips. The member puts who they are thanking for closed business. They put the information down. The slips are very easy to fill out. Or we have even created the online slips program in BNI Connect where you can start to thank people electronically. You can also start to track your return on investment for your own closed business. We have the mechanisms in place to allow people to perfect this system.

Ivan:
Something else, Priscilla, if you are listening to this: Not every member, not every region is on BNI Connect yet, but the overwhelming majority are. If you are not on BNI Connect. Ask, “When will we be on BNI Connect?”

John, is this part of the member module, or is it in the primary system?

John:
It is part of the member module, yes.

Ivan:
Okay, so if you are a member of regions that don’t, they have not flipped the switch to turn the member module on. So you may be on BNI Connect but still don’t have access to what John just described. If that is the case, pick up the phone and call your Executive Director and say, “When are you going to flip the switch for the member module? I would like to have it.”

Some of them are waiting because they want to work out the training before they just flip the switch. That is legitimate. I can tell you this. Sometime this year, we will flip in on for them. Sometime in 2013, it will be on for everyone on BNI Connect, even if the Director doesn’t flip it. So we will flip it towards the end of the year.

Everyone is going to have that online thing that John just described. Otherwise, the manual process is included in this podcast.

John, is there anything that you want to add to thank you for closed business?

John:
I think the key is just to do it. You don’t have to buy the definition, but you just have to do it for a number of reasons. It is going to help you with your success in BNI.

Ivan:
I think you are absolutely right. I’ll tell you this. You have taken what I did years ago for my dissertation and have really honed it down to details that I think will help members. I have been using this basic approach back when I did my dissertation. I’ll tell you this: the media loves to poke holes in numbers. For almost 20 years, I have been giving a less complex, simpler version of this. This is even more sophisticated, and the media has never poked a hole in this definition. They have always accepted it when they saw the data. So there is a history in using it.

John, you and your team have done a wonderful job in putting this together. Members, I really urge you to take a good look at this and then start to incorporate it. Members, if you don’t have access to these slips, please contact your local Director and tell them you want to participate in thank you for closed business. Please refer them to this podcast. We would love to work with them to make sure you have it in your chapter.

John, thanks a lot for being on the podcast today.

John:
Thank you very much.

Ivan:
Priscilla, back to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you very much. Well, I think that is it for this week. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by networkingnow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

Special Guest John Meyer Posted in Referrals, Special Guests Tagged with TYFCB

You may also like...

Episode 290: The Number 1 Trait of a Master Networker (Classic Podcast) »

Comments

    Solutions-focused comments are welcome. Dr. Misner reads every comment personally.

    All comments are moderated before posting. Profanity, personal attacks, and sales pitches will be deleted.

  1. Mario Tse says

    August 5, 2013 at 1:13 am

    As an NEC of Inspire Chapter in Hong Kong, I enjoyed sharing what I have learned from Podcast with my fellow members. I have one question regarding the right amount to be reported in the case of a property agency. I interpreted and told members to report in TYFCB only the amount of sales commission earned instead of the property’s price. This is to avoid from inflating the chapter’s total turnover by referral. Some members disagreed. What shall I do?

    Reply

  2. Dr. Ivan Misner says

    August 6, 2013 at 8:27 pm

    Mario, it sounds like you did it right. It should be the commission – not the gross sales price.

    Reply

  3. Daniel Osborne says

    November 11, 2013 at 2:43 pm

    So car salesmen should be reporting their commission as well then, correct? (in lieu of full purchase price of automobile)

    Reply

  4. John Meyer says

    November 12, 2013 at 9:34 am

    Daniel, you are correct. Car salesmen (or saleswomen) should be reporting their commission versus the full price of the car.

    Thank you for allowing the clarification.

    Reply

  5. Cathy Grammer says

    November 26, 2013 at 4:39 pm

    I am a Partylite Independent Consultant. I earn 25% of sales. When I fill out the green slips, I have been putting down the cost of the product w/o tax, s and h. For example an item costs 25.00, that is what I put down. Is that correct? Or do I put the total price of item? Or what I earn off of the sale? Thank you! Cathy Grammer of Shore Thing New Era, MI.

    Reply

  6. John Meyer says

    November 27, 2013 at 6:45 am

    Hello Cathy!

    It sounds like you have been reporting correctly. You should be putting down the 25% commission that you would receive. If that includes tax, s and h, then you can include that in the total. If your commission does not include those items, then you should not report them. I hope this answers your question. Thanks!

    Reply

  7. Rick says

    March 28, 2014 at 6:37 am

    So if our banker writes a home mortgage of $500,000, at 6% interest for 30 years, he should report $900,000 in TYFCB? Sounds high!

    Reply

  8. John Meyer says

    March 31, 2014 at 12:36 pm

    Hello Rick. Actually, a couple points of clarification. 1. Most (not all) mortgage bankers are paid commission, so they would notate their commission. 2. You are correct in that the interest is what should be reported as Thank You For Closed Business for installment loans. However, based on a mortgage loan calculator website, the interest for a $500,000 loan @ 6% for 30 Years would be roughly $579K. If the banker is not commissioned, that is the amount they should report. I hope this clears things up.
    Thanks!

    Reply

  9. Jim says

    February 12, 2015 at 6:23 pm

    In the attached pdf you stated that the banker should “Report the initial amount to open an account.” Does the mean the fees the bank charges the customer to open an account or the initial balance of the account, i.e., if the referral deposits $300,000 initially in the account, the banker should report that entire balance?

    Reply

  10. John Meyer says

    February 13, 2015 at 10:23 am

    Hello Jim

    Thank you for your question. The document that you are referring to has been updated as of February 2014. One of the changes was exactly what you are referring to. The current version states that the banker should report 10% of the initial deposit, so in your example, they would report $30,000. Feel free to email me at [email protected] for a copy of the current document or connect with your Executive Director.

    Respectfully

    John

    Reply

  11. rick883 says

    February 13, 2015 at 6:38 pm

    So if I refer a loan to the mortgage broker and she’s on commission and earns one point, or $5,000, that’s what she reports. But if I refer the same loan to a banker, they report the interest over the life of the loan, or $579,000. That’s wacky!

    Reply

  12. Ashley LeBlanc says

    November 27, 2015 at 1:02 pm

    Where can the banker PDF be located in its’ updated version?

    Reply

  13. creativesights01 says

    November 30, 2015 at 11:48 am

    What are the best practices for announcing/reporting TYFCB at a meeting? They’re not really a referral or a testimonial about another member’s products or services, but it seems a lot of people like to mention when they pass their slips at this point in the meeting.

    Reply

  14. John Meyer says

    November 30, 2015 at 12:47 pm

    This varies from chapter to chapter on how or if TYFCB’s are announced. Most chapter’s will request that the slips are passed at the same time referrals and/or testimonials are given during the meeting. One thing to make sure of though is that announcing TYFCB’s does not replace a member giving a referral or a testimonial.

    Reply

  15. John Meyer says

    November 30, 2015 at 12:52 pm

    Replying to Ashley Leblanc’s comment – Ashley, you can obtain a copy of the Slips Program document from your Executive Director or your Director Consultant. It fully explains the process.

    Reply

  16. Anne Scholtz says

    September 26, 2017 at 8:39 am

    Why doesn’t BNI Connect send a “Thank You” to the member who gave the referral when the information is entered, similar to the email that is sent when a member gives a referral? It would be a great double-check on the TYFCB accuracy, and an additional nice way to thank the referring member.

    Reply

    • Jeremy Walsh says

      September 26, 2017 at 5:11 pm

      @ANNE –

      Dr. Misner asked me to step in here as the Director of Support for BNI Connect.

      You are right and I agree – it would be a great accountability helper to get this type of notification. However, it is something that we are legally prevented from doing.

      The TYFCB slips online was modeled directly inline with the official paper slips program in BNI. In the paper version, the TYFCB is an anonymous entry (for many reasons, some of them for client confidentiality).

      From the BNI Slips document (https://support.bniconnect.com/entries/40561363-BNI-Slips-Program-Overview):
      Note: By assigning this amount to the person who gave the referral resulting in the business, this
      will preserve confidentiality of each individual’s income. In addition, this will show each member’s value as a “referral giver” to the other members of the chapter.

      Although a notification and\or seeing both sides of the transaction may be desired by some, it could be “illegal” for others. Most notably this could cause doctors, lawyers and other professionals to not be able to participate in the TYFCB process.

      In the meantime, the best way to make another member aware is by letting them know and, if there is mutual agreement, announcing it during the “I have” portion of the meeting. In addition, BNI Connect offers a voluntary\optional REFERRAL TRACKING PROGRAM (https://bniconnect.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/articles/219067607-Referral-Tracking-Sheet) that allows you to update the status of your received referrals. That status is shared with your referral partner so that they know that the referral was indeed closed and can therefore assume you have submitted the TYFCB for it while still maintaining the legal distance required.

      Hope this helps!

      Reply

  17. Pierpaolo Testella says

    May 23, 2018 at 9:26 am

    I’m a little bit confused about TYFCB reporting. I’m an insurance broker and I usually have reported my gross commission figures, but this morning someone told me I have to report the premiums amount, because BNI changed the rules on this topic. I’d like to keep on reporting commissions and not premiums amount as usual. Am I right or wrong?

    Reply

  18. Jeremy Walsh says

    May 23, 2018 at 9:43 am

    Pierpaolo,

    Here is a guide to the slips program, including TYFCB reporting:
    https://support.bniconnect.com/hc/en-us/articles/219067637-BNI-Slips-Program-Overview

    Here is an excerpt from that document:

    Commission Based Sales
    If you are commissioned, put down your gross commission figure. Examples:

    Insurance: P&C, Realtors, Insurance: Health Care, Mortgages, Online Sales (such as greeting cards), Investments, Insurance: Life, Payroll Services

    Hope this helps!

    Reply

  19. Isabel Dias Rodrigues says

    October 5, 2018 at 5:09 pm

    I’m a little bit confused about TYFCB reporting. I’m a real estate developer. If a member from a real estate agency which is a BNI member sells one of my houses priced 250000 euros, which is the value that I register?
    And in that case he/she registers the commission?
    Many thanks in advance

    Reply

  20. Ivan Misner says

    October 6, 2018 at 12:16 pm

    It is the commission – not the sales price.

    Reply

  21. Ricky Trione says

    November 16, 2018 at 2:22 pm

    This Podcast was very helpful to me with the Closed Business!

    Reply

  22. Ashwin Acharya says

    June 26, 2019 at 9:33 am

    Hi,

    Can I send out an advertisement for a tender to a fellow member and expect him pass a TYFCB to me. Or should I pass a TYFCB to someone who just sent me an Ad in a news paper?

    Reply

  23. Rachelle Nelson says

    March 16, 2021 at 1:05 pm

    I think I may have been submitting my TYCB incorrectly to this point. I was told that for a direct sales company, that I need to put down the entire sale less s&h. That’s an inflated number for me. I would get up to 35% of that. Should I be using my percentage of the sale or the full sale?

    Reply

    • Ivan Misner says

      March 16, 2021 at 5:35 pm

      Rachelle, take a look at the PDF that is mentioned in that podcast. Use your best judgement about your situation given that PDF.

      https://www.bnipodcast.com/2017/09/13/episode-524-thank-closed-business-classic-podcast/

      Reply

  24. VE Ramanathan says

    April 25, 2021 at 9:59 am

    we have members in used car buying & selling category. When he buys a car from one of our member who has to mention TYFCB – the User Car buyer or the member who sells the car. Also what amount to be entered in TYFCB – the total purchase amount ?

    Need some info on the same

    Reply
